coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots rapid and selective detection of mercury ion and biothiol and construction of an IMPLICATION logic gate
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Sen
Li, Xinzong
Yang, Hua
Chen, Xiaoqing
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by adjusting the ratio of carbon sources (citric acid) and nitrogen sources (ethanolamine). This N-CDs fluorescent probe had been successfully applied to rapidly, sensitively and selectively determine the concentration of mercury ion (Hg2+) with a linear range of 0.1–5.0 μM and a limit of detection of 0.019 μM. In this work, it was demonstrated that N-CDs show the fluorescence response toward Hg2+ when the carboxyl groups of citric acid reacts completely with the amino groups of ethanolamine. In addition, the quenched N-CDs/Hg2+ system could be recovered by biothiol, such as cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH), exhibiting a sensitive response toward Cys and Hcy over GSH in HEPES buffer. N-CDs could be used as a potential fluorescent bioimaging material due to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Furthermore, an IMPLICATION logic gate based on Hg2+ and biothiol was constructed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon dots
Mercury ion
Biothiol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hg2+ detection in of real water samples
Mechanism and characterization of the N-CDs
The application of N-CDs in detection Hg2+
The application of N-CDs/Hg2+ in detection
Construction of a fluorescent IMPLICATION logic gate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475152 and 21576296 ) and Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety (No. 2018TP1003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-CDs and its sensing mechanism for Hg2+ and biothiol. (B) Fluorescence intensity changed when add Hg2+ into three kinds of carbon dots solution.

(A) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-CDs and its sensing mechanism for Hg2+ and biothiol. The quenching efficiency of N-CDs as a function of (B) ratio of CA to EA; (C) temperature of the hydrothermal method. (F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 446 nm in the presence or absence of Hg2+, respectively.).

(A) TEM and HRTEM image (inset) images of N-CDs. (B) Time-resolved fluorescence decay of N-CDs, N-CDs/Hg2+ and N-CDs/Hg2+ + biothiol. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs and N-CDs/Hg2+. (D) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of as-synthesized N-CDs. C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D) spectra of the prepared N-CDs, respectively.

(A) Comparison of FL intensities of N-CDs upon addition of different metal ions all at the concentrations of 100 μM. (B) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of increasing Hg2+ concentrations (0.1–15.0 μM) in the 0.01 M HEPES at pH 7.4. (C) The relationship between F-F0 and Hg2+ from 0.1 to 15.0 μM. (D) The relationship between F-F0 and Hg2+ from 0.1 to 5.0 μM. (F and F0 are the FL intensities of N-CDs at 446 nm in the presence and absence of Hg2+, respectively.).

(A) Comparison of FL intensities of N-CDs/Hg2+ upon addition of different amino acids and anion ions all at the concentrations of 100 μM. (B) Plot of the FL intensity as a function of Cys concentration. (C) Plot of the FL intensity as a function of Hcy concentration. (D) The relationship between F-F0 and GSH from 0.1 to 100 μM. (Insets in (B) and (C) show the linear relationship between F-F0 and the analyte concentration).
Fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells. (A) represent the bright-field images of HeLa cell. (B) Images of cells incubated with N-CDs. (C) Overlaid fluorescence images of HeLa cells.
IMPLICATION logic gate designed with Hg2+ and biothiol as inputs. The output is the measurement of FL intensity of N-CDs: (A) symbol, (B) truth table.
Comparison of detection performance of different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.
Comparison of different methods for biothiols detection.

row.entry: A fluorescent probe based on fluorescein

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Hg2+ in natural water samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots rapid and selective detection of mercury ion and biothiol and construction of an IMPLICATION logic gate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by adjusting the ratio of carbon sources (citric acid) and nitrogen sources (ethanolamine). This N-CDs fluorescent probe had been successfully applied to rapidly, sensitively and selectively determine the concentration of mercury ion (Hg2+) with a linear range of 0.1–5.0 μM and a limit of detection of 0.019 μM. In this work, it was demonstrated that N-CDs show the fluorescence response toward Hg2+ when the carboxyl groups of citric acid reacts completely with the amino groups of ethanolamine. In addition, the quenched N-CDs/Hg2+ system could be recovered by biothiol, such as cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH), exhibiting a sensitive response toward Cys and Hcy over GSH in HEPES buffer. N-CDs could be used as a potential fluorescent bioimaging material due to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Furthermore, an IMPLICATION logic gate based on Hg2+ and biothiol was constructed.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as stable light-emitting carbon nanomaterials [1] with quasi-spherical structures smaller than 10 nm have received considerable attention owing to their unique properties, such as biocompatibility [2], low toxicity [3] and high photostability [4]. In particular, CDs are of significance as optical detection probes [5], bioimaging [6,7], biosensor [8], nanomedicine [9], photocatalytic [10] etc. Recently, it was observed that metal ions could turn off the fluorescence of CDs and specific chelators could restore the fluorescence through forming complexes with the metal ions. Based on this mechanism, a variety of important biological fluorescence “off–on” CDs sensors were designed for the detection of metal ions and chelators. For instance, it was reported that Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs and the recovery of fluorescence was obtained by the addition of pyrophosphate anions (PPi) [11]. It was also confirmed that the fluorescence of PNCQDs could be turned off by adding Cr6+ and the “on” fluorescence was fulfilled through the introduction of ascorbic acid (AA) [12].

Ion pollution including Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+ and so on has become a critical worldwide issue due to the severe risks in organisms and environment. In particular, there are an array of analytical techniques for the detection of Hg2+, such as inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [13], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [14], and atomic fluorescence spectrometry [15]. Generally, all of these analytical techniques are quite expensive and difficult to handle, which greatly limit their wide applications. Currently, a lot of efforts are dedicated to developing operationally simple and inexpensive methods. Compared with traditional methods, fluorescent probes have attracted considerable attention for their simplicity, efficiency, and high sensitivity and selectivity. As a consequence, many fluorescence probes have been designed to detect Hg2+ in aqueous solutions. For instance, Maity et al. successfully synthesized N,N-bis(2-(pyridin-2-ylmethoxy)ethyl)aniline receptor (BDPS) to selectively sense Hg2+. Given the fact that fluorescent organic molecules usually show cytotoxicity and are easily bleached [16], alternative fluorescent probes have been developed in the detection of Hg2+. For instance, Wu et al. employed green fluorescent nitrogen-rich quantum dots to detect Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous media [17]. Despite the encouraging advance in the detection of mercury ion, less attention was paid to the mercury ion response time, which would allow the development of rapid detection method. Accordingly, it is high desirable to pursue a rapid and highly selective fluorescence detection method to facilitate the detection of mercury ions.

On the basis of the strong interaction between Hg2+ and biothiol, a fluorescent CQDs probe for the detection of biothiol was constructed through the “off-on” fluorescence. It is well known that cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) play important roles in the regulation of numerous physiological and pathological processes [18,19]. Among them, Cys is an essential amino acid in human protein synthesis [20], which is associated with various human diseases such as skin lesions, hair depigmentation, liver damage, retarded growth rate and lethargy [21]. Thus, it is of significant interest to develop highly selective methods to monitor and quantitate biothiol under physiological conditions in clinical medicine and investigations aiming at probing cellular functions.

In this work, we synthesized three kinds of carbon dots (N-CDs, CDs and N,N-CDs) via a simple hydrothermal method by employing citric acid as carbon source and three kinds of small molecules (ethanol amine, ethanediol and ethidene diamine) as the dopant. It was found that the fluorescence of N-CDs were quenched quickly in only half a minute by Hg2+ via the formation of N-CDs/Hg2+. On the basis of the rapid fluorescence quenching by Hg2+, a novel sensor for rapid and selective detection of Hg2+ was established. Upon adding biothiol, the fluorescence of the system could be effectively recovered through the complexation of biothiol with Hg2+. Moreover, the as-prepared N-CDs were applied as fluorescent probes to detect Hg2+ in real water samples.

Citric acid (CA), ethanol amine (EA), ethanediol, ethidene diamine, quinine sulfate, cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH) and 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-Piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were bought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm−1 by a Molecular water purification system.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was measured by HT7700 (Hitachi, Japan). The Infrared spectra were measured by IR Prestige-21 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The UV–Vis spectra were recorded using a UV-2600 (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was measured by Fluo Time 100 (PicoQuant, Germany).

Initially, CA (0.4200 g) and EA (625 μL) were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. The crude product was collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation at 9000 rpm. The product, which was canary yellow and transparent, was subjected to dialysis in order to obtain the N-CDs. After evaporating the solvent and further drying under vacuum, the purified N-CDs samples were finally procured.

N-CDs were adjusted to a concentration of 50 μg mL−1 and 100 µM aqueous solutions containing thirteen different metal ions (Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Ag+) were prepared respectively, and then the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded after 1 min. To study the detection range of Hg2+, 2 mL of N-CDs solution (0.01 M HEPES, pH 7.4) was added to a 3 mL quartz cell, 4 μL Hg2+ solutions (0.01 M HEPES, pH 7.4) with varied concentrations (50 µM, 500 µM and 5000 µM) were added to the system. The fluorescence spectra were recorded (excited at 360 nm) after 1 min.

2 mL of N-CDs/Hg2+ solution (0.01 M HEPES, pH 7.4) was added to a 3 mL quartz cell. As for the detection of biothiol (Cys, Hcy and GSH), 4 μL biothiol solutions with varied concentrations (50 µM, 500 µM and 5000 µM) were added to N-CDs/Hg2+ system. The fluorescence spectra were recorded (excited at 360 nm) after 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ detection in of real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the proposed method, real water samples from the Xiangjiang river in Hunan province and tap water of laboratory were analyzed. All the water samples collected were filtered using 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities. Different concentrations of Hg2+ were added to the as-prepared water samples and then these samples were analyzed using the N-CDs probe.

To investigate the toxicity of the prepared N-CDs and N-CDs/Hg2+probe in living cells, HeLa cells were incubated in 96-well plate and grown in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Different concentrations of the N-CDs and N-CDs/Hg2+ (0–100 μg mL−1) were added for further incubation for 24 h. Then, 100 μL of the new culture medium containing methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) (10 μL, 5 mg mL−1) was added, followed by incubating for 4 h to form violet formazan dye, the media were removed and 100 μL of dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were added into each well to dissolve the formazan. The absorbance optical density of the purple formazan at 490 nm was measured on the enzyme-linked immunosorbent detector.

The potential of N-CDs in bio-labeling was preliminarily evaluated by their cellular imaging in Hela cells. In brief, HeLa cells were grown in coverglass bottom dishes (29 mm × 10 mm) with DMEM supplemented contain 10% FBS for 24 h. The cells were washed with HEPES for three times and N-ethylmaleimide (NEM, 500 μM, a scavenger of biothiol) was then added [22]. Subsequently, a solution of N-CDs (50 μg mL−1) was added after 0.5 h, respectively, and afterwards the cells were incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 1.0 h. After being washed with HEPES three times, Hg2+ (10 μM) and Cys (10 μM) were continuously added during the observation respectively while the photos were simultaneously taken [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and characterization of the N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this research, CA was selected as a carbon source for preparing carbon dots, while EA, ethanediol and ethidene diamine served as dopants respectively ( Fig. 1A). Three kinds of carbon dots including nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), carbon dots (CDs) and nitrogen-rich carbon dots (N,N-CDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method. The amount of nitrogen introduced into the prepared N,N-CDs and N-CDs was surveyed by XPS (Fig. S1). The results show the success of the doping of nitrogen. As shown in Fig. 1B, the solution of Hg2+ with same concentration was added into three kinds of carbon dots solutions with the same fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence of CDs did not changed obviously within 10 min, suggesting that Hg2+ was unable to quench the fluorescence of CDs. N-CDs and N,N-CDs had obvious response to Hg2+. Fluorescence intensity decreased promptly after the addition of Hg2+ into the solution of N-CDs, and maintained a balance within half a minute. However, the fluorescence of N,N-CDs didn’t arrive to the balance within 10 min. Therefore, N-CDs can be utilized to detect mercury ions quickly.

As shown in Fig. 2A, the N-CDs were prepared using citric acid as carbon sources and ethanolamine acted as nitrogen sources. The fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by adding Hg2+, in which Hg2+ might be bound to the surface of the N-CDs to generate N-CDs/Hg2+ complex. This complexation was destroyed by the stronger complexation between biothiol and Hg2+, resulting in restoring the fluorescence. The fluorescent behavior of the N-CDs was affected by numerous factors, such as ratio of carbon source and nitrogen source, temperature of hydrothermal method and reaction time. Thus, the preparation conditions of N-CDs should be optimized toward achieving N-CDs with good fluorescence properties. As shown in Fig. 2B, when the ratio of carbon source and nitrogen source increased to 1:5 from 1:1, the fluorescence of N-CDs (F0) was increased. Meanwhile, the quenching efficiency of N-CDs (F0-F) was significantly increased when the ratio of carbon source and nitrogen source increased above 1:3. And as shown in Fig. S2, the FT-IR results show that the peak strength of functional group is basically unchanged when the proportion increases to 1:3. So the carboxyl groups of citric acid and amino groups of ethanolamine might form amide bonds completely when the nitrogen source was increased to 1:3 [24]. The temperature of the hydrothermal method was also optimized (Fig. 2C). The temperatures investigated in this study were 140, 150, 160, 170, and 180 °C while the ratio of carbon source to nitrogen source was kept at 1:5 and the time for the reaction was 6 h. The result clearly demonstrated that the quenching efficiency of N-CDs (F0-F) was significantly increased upon increasing temperature to 160 °C. Though high temperature was conducive to the reaction, the surface group of the N-CDs would be damaged when the temperature was too high [9]. As shown in Fig. S3, it can be observed that the fluorescence of N-CDs (F0) was increased when the reaction time was increased up to 6 h. However, the quenching efficiency of N-CDs (F0-F) remained unchanged since the reaction was almost complete in 6 h [25]. In conclusions, the optimum experimental conditions are as follows: 1 mmol citric acid, 5 mmol ethanolamine, 160 °C and 6 h. And the QY of the N-CDs was measured to be 14.2% using quinine sulfate as reference [11] (Fig. S4).

The morphology of the prepared N-CDs was explored by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which reveals that N-CDs exhibit good dispersibility as shown in Fig. 3A and the N-CDs are spherical with an average particle size about 2.25 nm (inset of Fig. 3A). As displayed in the HRTEM images (inset of Fig. 3A) of one nanoparticle reveals that N-CDs had a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (100) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon as reported [26].

In Fig. 3B, the fluorescence decay time of the N-CDs is 5.25 ns. Lifetimes decreased to 4.63 ns with the addition of Hg2+ into the solution of N-CDs. This minor change in lifetime suggests that it is static quenching. Then the lifetime returned to 5.07 ns when biothiol was added into the system of N-CDs/Hg2+, presumably due to the stronger interaction between Hg2+ and biothiol and the resulting separation of Hg2+ from the surface of N-CDs. We also studied the effect of temperature on quenching constant (Fig. S5). The results show that temperature essentially is impactful to the quenching process. In Fig. 3C, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum show that the absorption peak at 345 nm (black line) might be assigned to n-π * transition of the C˭O bond. The peak moved to 335 nm (red line) from 345 nm when Hg2+ was added into the solution of N-CDs. Consequently, it could be rationalized that N-CDs were quenched by Hg2+ in a manner of static quenching.

As show in Fig. 3D, the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs reveals a broad peak at 3420 cm−1 correlating with stretching vibrations of –NH or –OH. The peak at 1564 cm−1 is attributed to C˭O stretching in the conjugated structure of secondary amide [27]. The peak at 1381 cm−1 is ascribed to C–N stretching vibration of amide. The peak at 1083 cm−1 is ascribed to C–O stretching vibration of hydroxy [28].

The elemental composition has been determined from XPS analysis ( Fig. 4). Three distinct peaks around 286.0 eV, 399.0 eV, 530.0 eV in Fig. 4A shows three characteristic peaks of C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively [9]. As shown in Fig. 4B, the C1s XPS spectrum could be deconvoluted into three components C-C (283.5 eV), C-N (284.9 eV), C-O (286.7 eV) and C˭O (287.6 eV) functional groups [29–31] respectively. Deconvolution of N1s spectra in Fig. 4C reveal two relative nitrogen species of -NH (399.5 eV) and C-N (400.8 eV) [29]. As shown in Fig. 4D, a deconvoluted O1s spectrum shows a peak at 530.8 eV (C-O) and 532.2 eV (C˭O) [25,28].

The excitation spectrum (black line) and several emission spectra under various excitation wavelengths that range from 340 nm to 380 nm are shown in Fig. S6, manifesting that N-CDs exhibit a maximum emission with at 456 nm with excitation wavelength at 360 nm. It is worth noting that the variation in λex from 340 to 380 nm for N-CDs only resulted in the rapid decrease in the intensity of λem, without shifting the peak position. This stemmed from the highly homogeneous surface structure and narrow size distribution of N-CDs [32,33]. Given the fact that environments with varied pH values were often encountered in practical applications, the effect of pH of the background buffer on the FL intensity of the N-CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, N-CDs show the stronger fluorescence intensity under neutral alkaline environment. For a decrease in solution pH from 6 to 2, the protonation degree of the N-CDs gradually increases, producing higher concentrations of protonated nitrogen and oxygen atoms on the surface of the N-CDs [34,35]. The FL intensity was very stable even in solution with high ionic strength even to 1.0 M NaCl (Fig. S8), indicating that N-CDs had excellent anti-interference ability. All these results indicate that the N-CDs have superior optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of N-CDs in detection Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the sensitivity of N-CDs for Hg2+ detection was estimated, and the relative fluorescence intensities in the presence of common metal ions including Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Ag+ under the same experimental conditions (100 μM interferents) were determined. As demonstrated in Fig. 5A, the FL intensity was the feeblest when Hg2+ was introduced to the N-CDs composite solution. The FL intensity gradually decreased with an increase in Hg2+ concentration (Fig. 5B). The FL intensity exhibited relationships with the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0.1–15.0 μM (Fig. 5C) and exhibited linear relationship F0-F = 67.171 [Hg2+] (μM) + 15.479 with the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0.1–5.0 μM (R2 = 0.993) (Fig. 5D). The detection limit of Hg2+ was 0.019 μM based on the equation 3σ/S (σ is standard deviation of background recorded (N = 10) and S is the slope of the linear fit). As we can see from Table 1, this detection method is comparable or even superior to other methods in the detection of Hg2+. The results show that our method has lower LOD of the carbon dots sensor of Hg2+ than that in ref. S2, S3, S5 and S6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of N-CDs/Hg2+ in detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent probes of N-CDs/Hg2+ solution (10 μg mL−1 N-CDs and 50 μM Hg2+) with the addition of different biomolecules (Cys, Hcy, GSH, Thr, His, Trp, Lys, Gly, Glu, Arg and Phe) and anion ions (sulfion and iodide ions) were studied. As shown in Fig. 6A, the quenched FL intensity could be recovered by adding biothiol (Cys, Hcy and GSH) into the system of N-CDs/Hg2+. Only in the presence of biothiol (Cys, Hcy and GSH), a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed and the intensity was recovered to 80.8%, 76.6% and 45.7%, respectively (Fig. S9). Due to the greater steric hindrance of GSH, the response of GSH was in fact smaller than that of Cys [36]. In Fig. 6B, a linear calibration in a range of 0.1–10 μM (R2 = 0.962) was constructed, and the regression equation was F-F0 = 6.626 [Cys] (μM) – 0.532 (inset of Fig. 6B). The detection limit of Cys was calculated to be 0.097 μM based on the equation 3σ/S. In Fig. 6C, a linear calibration in a range of 0.5–10 μM (R2 = 0.987) was constructed, and the regression equation was F-F0 = 14.083 [Hcy] (μM) – 5.745 (inset of Fig. 6C). The detection limit of Hcy was calculated to be 0.045 μM (3σ/S). In Fig. 6D, there was a linear calibration (R2 = 0.989) between the relative intensity and the concentration of GSH. And the regression equation was F-F0 = 3.760 [GSH] (μM) + 1.169. The LOD for GSH was determined to be 0.169 μM (3σ/S), and the detective range was from 0.1 to 100 μM. Although the method can detect biothiol, it cannot differentiate between Cys, Hcy and GSH. Such detection method was comparable or superior to other methods for the detection of biothiol ( Table 2). The result show that N-CDs/Hg2+ has lower LOD of Cys than that in ref. S11 and S9 and wider linear range than that in ref. S7.

Samples of river water from the Xiangjiang river in Hunan province and tap water of laboratory were used for testing Hg2+ by the new N-CDs fluorescence probes. Table 3 shows the satisfactory recoveries were achieved ranging from 96.0% to 104.5%. These results exemplify that the N-CDs fluorescence probes can be applicable for environmental water samples.

The cytotoxicity of the N-CDs and N-CDs/Hg2+ fluorescence probe was studied via MTT assay. It was observed that the viability of the HeLa cells was over 80% even when the concentration of N-CDs and N-CDs /Hg2+ fluorescence probe was as high as 100 µg mL−1 (Fig. S10). The result indicates that the N-CDs and N-CDs/Hg2+ fluorescence probe have low cytotoxicity and great potential applications in the fluorescence imaging of live cells.

Fig. 7 is the image of living cells under a fluorescence inversion microscope system. Fig. 7A shows the images of HeLa cells incubated with N-CDs (50 μg mL−1) for 1.0 h under bright field. The HeLa cells show blue fluorescence under the excitation wavelength (Fig. 7B). As shown in Fig. S11B, the fluorescence intensity decreased when Hg2+ was added into the N-CDs (Fig. S11A). And the fluorescence intensity would be then recovered upon the addition of Cys (Fig. S11C). All these results demonstrated that the N-CDs could be used as a remarkable fluorescent bioimaging material because of their low toxicity and good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a fluorescent IMPLICATION logic gate
ce:section.ce:para: In IMPLICATION operations, the output is “0” only when input 1 is equal to “1”, and the input 2 has to be equal to “0”. Otherwise, the rest output is “1”. Fluorescence of the N-CDs permitted the design of IMPLICATION logic gate based on Boolean operations when Hg2+ and biothiol as inputs ( Fig. 8). For inputs, the presence of Hg2+ or biothiol is considered to be the “1” state and their absence as “0”. The symbol of the IMPLICATION logic gate is shown in Fig. 8A. The truth table contains the details of all combinations is represented in Fig. 8B, showing that the input of Hg2+ and absence biothiol (1/0), the FL intensity of N-CDs are quenched, and the output is “0”. In all other cases, the outputs are “1”.

In conclusion, we developed a facile and low-cost one-pot hydrothermal protocol for synthesizing N-CDs. The as-prepared N-CDs not only displayed superior dispersibility, great photoluminescence and excellent stability, but also exhibited rapid, sensitive and selective response toward Hg2+ over other metal ions. The FL intensity exhibited linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ in a range of 0.1–5.0 μM with LOD of 0.019 μM. Biothiol recovered the fluorescence of N-CDs/Hg2+ system through the complexation of biothiol with Hg2+, in which the restoration ability follows the order of Cys > Hcy > GSH. Moreover, N-CDs have been applied for imaging in HeLa cells, and it provides a possibility to detect endogenous biothiol quantitatively. Finally, an IMPLICATION logic gate was constructed to mimic this research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475152 and 21576296) and Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety (No. 2018TP1003).

ce:displayed-quote.ce:simple-para: The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.09.114.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
J. Mater. Chem. C
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Anal. Chem.
Bi-functional fluorescent polymer dots: a one-step synthesis via controlled hydrothermal treatment and application as probes for the detection of temperature and Fe3+
J. Mater. Chem. C
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticle modulated turn-on fluorescent probes for histidine detection and its imaging in living cells
Nanoscale
Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensing probes for Ag+ ions detection
Sens. Actuators B: Chem.
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Chem. Soc. Rev.
Carbon dots with red emission for sensing of Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+ and their bioapplications in vitro and in vivo
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
